elements.sent-2411-038: Graph clustering is an unsupervised machine learning method that partitions the nodes in a graph into different groups.
elements.sent-2411-039: Despite achieving significant progress in exploiting both attributed and structured data information, graph clustering methods often face practical challenges related to data isolation.
elements.sent-2411-040: Moreover, the absence of collaborative methods for graph clustering limits their effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-041: In this paper, we propose a collaborative graph clustering framework for attributed graphs, supporting attributed graph clustering over vertically partitioned data with different participants holding distinct features of the same data.
elements.sent-2411-042: Our method leverages a novel technique that reduces the sample space, improving the efficiency of the attributed graph clustering method.
elements.sent-2411-043: Furthermore, we compare our method to its centralized counterpart under a proximity condition, demonstrating that the successful local results of each participant contribute to the overall success of the collaboration.
elements.sent-2411-044: We fully implement our approach and evaluate its utility and efficiency by conducting experiments on four public datasets.
elements.sent-2411-045: The results demonstrate that our method achieves comparable accuracy levels to centralized attributed graph clustering methods.
elements.sent-2411-046: Our collaborative graph clustering framework provides an efficient and effective solution for graph clustering challenges related to data isolation.

elements.sent-2411-071: Our contributions can be summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-072: We introduce $\name$, a communication-efficient collaborative learning framework to solve attributed graph clustering in vertically collaborative settings where different participants sharing a common graph structure hold different features of the same data.
elements.sent-2411-073: We improve the communication efficiency by communicating only the local clusters, making the communication cost \revisionminor{reduce from $O(n)$ to $O(k^3)$, where k is the number of local clusters and $n$ is the size of dataset .}

elements.sent-2411-076: Specifically, the proposed ``restricted proximity condition`` is a compromise between the conditions proposed by~\cite{awasthi2012improved and~\cite{kumar2010clustering .}}

elements.sent-2411-113: Given an attributed non-directed graph $\mathcal{G=(V, E, \X)}$, the normalized graph Laplacian is defined as \begin{equation}L_s = I-D^{-\frac{1}{2}}AD^{-\frac{1}{2}},\end{equation} where $A$ is an adjacency matrix and $D$ is a diagonal matrix called the degree matrix.
elements.sent-2411-114: Since the normalized graph Laplacian $L_s$ is a real symmetric matrix, $L_s$ can be eigen-decomposed as $L_s=Q\Lambda Q^{-1}$, where $\Lambda =diag(\lambda_1, \dots, \lambda_n)$ is a diagonal matrix composed of the eigenvalues, and $Q = [q_1, \dots, q_n]$ is an orthogonal matrix composed of the associated orthogonal eigenvectors.
elements.sent-2411-115: The smoothness of a graph signal $f$ can be deduced similar to the classic Fourier transform.
elements.sent-2411-116: The classical Fourier transform of a function $f$ is, \begin{equation}\hat{f}(\xi) = \int_{\mathbb{R}}f(t)e^{-2\pi i \xi t}dt,\end{equation} which are the eigenfunctions of the one-dimensional Laplace operator, \begin{equation}-\Delta(e^{-2\pi i \xi t}) = -\frac{\partial^2}{\partial t^2}e^{-2\pi i \xi t} = (2\pi i \xi)^2e^{-2\pi i \xi t}.\end{equation}.
